description_long: This sand patch consists of a flat, irregularly shaped section of brown terrain with a slightly raised edge and a deep, dark crevice running diagonally across its surface. The main surface is dotted with lighter patches and subtle textural variations, suggesting a mix of compacted sand and small stones. The crevice appears to be a natural depression or erosion channel, with steeper sides and a shadowed interior. The patch's boundaries are uneven, with one side featuring a pronounced cut or drop-off. The coloration is predominantly earthy brown, with some lighter and darker areas adding visual depth. This object would typically be found in an urban park, roadside, or construction site where sand and soil accumulate and erode.
description: A flat, irregular brown sand patch with a deep diagonal crevice and scattered lighter spots, featuring uneven edges and a pronounced cut.
description_view_0: From this angle, the sand patch's flat surface and the deep crevice cutting through the right side are clearly visible, with the irregular edge most prominent in the foreground.
description_view_1: This view highlights the crevice running from left to right, showing the depth and shadow within, and the raised edge on the left side.
description_view_2: Seen from this perspective, the sand patch appears mostly flat, with the crevice receding into the background and the surface texture more apparent.
description_view_3: Here, the raised edge and the start of the crevice are visible at the back, while the main surface extends forward with subtle color variations.
attribute_location: urban park, roadside, or construction site
attribute_boundary_definition: The boundaries are irregular and somewhat abrupt, with some edges appearing sharply cut and others blending more gradually into the surrounding terrain.
attribute_presence_of_debris: minimal, with only small natural inclusions like pebbles visible
attribute_integration_with_surrounding_s: The sand patch appears partially blended with its surroundings, with some edges merging into adjacent surfaces and others more distinctly separated.
